items.q: Was the Big Bang an explosion of matter into pre-existing space?
items.correct: No — it was the expansion of space itself, beginning from a hot dense state; there was no 'outside' for matter to expand into.
items.misconception: Yes — it was a giant explosion at a point, and galaxies are still flying outwards from it.
items.random_wrong: Yes — it was a bomb-like event that destroyed an earlier universe.
items.why: Pop-science imagery of a fiery explosion encourages the wrong mental model; in fact every point in the universe was once 'the centre'.

items.q: When energy is 'lost' as heat due to friction, what has really happened?
items.correct: Energy has been transferred to the thermal store of the surroundings — it's not destroyed, just dissipated.
items.misconception: Energy has been used up and no longer exists.
items.random_wrong: Energy has been converted into mass following E=mc^2.
items.why: Everyday language ('used up', 'lost') and bills ('energy used') reinforce the idea energy is consumed, contradicting conservation.

items.q: Why does Faraday's law include a minus sign (Lenz's law)?
items.correct: The induced current flows in a direction that opposes the change in flux that produced it — a consequence of energy conservation.
items.misconception: The minus sign is a mathematical convention with no physical meaning.
items.random_wrong: The minus sign means the current flows backwards through the wire.
items.why: Students treat the minus sign as a notational quirk rather than encoding Lenz's law, the deep statement that induced effects oppose their cause.

items.q: Redshift of distant galaxies is evidence that:
items.why: It's the universe is expanding. “the universe is contracting” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The Sun is currently in which stage?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Main sequence. “Protostar” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If the universe is 13.8 billion years old, how many seconds is that? (1 year ≈ 3.15×10⁷ s; answer in 10¹⁷ s)
items.why: Exact answer: 4.35 x10^17 s. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
